answer:
**The work is *Kunjarakarna Dharmakathana* (by Mpu Dusun), Chapter 1.**[^1]

This Old Javanese (Kawi) Buddhist narrative recounts how the *gandharva* king Pūrṇavijaya, due to past evil deeds, falls into the Avīci hell (abode of Yama). By the Buddha's grace after hearing the *dharma*, his suffering is limited to nine nights; on the tenth, meditation with a pure mind reverses the hellish torments.[^2]

The cauldron (*Gomukha*) transformation is described in Canto 27:

> Now, he who was in hell, it is told, on his ninth night,  
> There he became mindful, and the sharpness of the cauldron felt as nothing.  
> When the tenth night arrived, he held to a meditation of pure mind.  
> The cauldron was shattered, its fire died, transformed into a jeweled lotus pond.[^3]

The nearby sword-tree (*asivarṇa*) transformation follows immediately in the same canto:

> And the sword-tree became a lovely Pārijāta, all glorious.  
> Its fruits were flawless gems, Kaustubha jewels, its flowers golden necklaces.  
> Its soft leaves were a fragrant footstool, its blood-sap exceedingly sweet-smelling.  
> It ceased to be foul, sharp-smelling, and pungent; all its qualities were reversed, bringing bliss.[^4]

[^1]: *Kunjarakarna Dharmakathana*, [Chapter 1](/works/unknown-kunjarakarna-dharmakathana-894f4a/01/translation/).
[^2]: *Kunjarakarna Dharmakathana*, [p-c6e7d8](/works/unknown-kunjarakarna-dharmakathana-894f4a/01/translation/#p-c6e7d8).
[^3]: *Kunjarakarna Dharmakathana*, [p-1353c8](/works/unknown-kunjarakarna-dharmakathana-894f4a/01/translation/#p-1353c8).
[^4]: *Kunjarakarna Dharmakathana*, [p-5cee32](/works/unknown-kunjarakarna-dharmakathana-894f4a/01/translation/#p-5cee32).
